lesson.title: Three-Phase Power Systems
lesson.explanation: Three-phase supply provides 400V between phases (230V phase-to-neutral) and is used for industrial motors, large commercial buildings, and high-power equipment. Star connection: 3 live wires + neutral, each phase 120° apart. Delta connection: 3 live wires, no neutral, used for motors. Power factor correction capacitors reduce reactive power and avoid Eskom penalties. Three-phase motors are more efficient and self-starting compared to single-phase.

examples.problem: Practical scenario in Three-Phase Power Systems
examples.answer: Follow Three-Phase Power Systems best practices

practice_problems.question: A factory has a three-phase supply (400V line-to-line). They want to install a 15kW motor. Calculate the full-load current for both star and delta connections. Which connection gives lower starting current?
practice_problems.answer: Full-load current calculation (assuming power factor 0.85 and efficiency 0.90): Input power = 15kW / 0.90 = 16.67kW. For three-phase: I = P / (√3 x V x pf). Delta connection (400V across each winding): I = 16,667 / (√3 x 400 x 0.85) = 16,667 / 589.9 = 28.3A. Star connection (230V across each winding): line current is the same as phase current, but the motor develops less torque. Star-delta starting: motor starts in STAR (lower voltage per winding = 230V, starting current reduced to 1/3 of delta starting current), then switches to DELTA for full-speed operation. Star starting current ≈ 28.3 x 2 (typical starting multiplier in star) vs delta starting current ≈ 28.3 x 6. Star starting gives approximately 1/3 the starting current of direct delta starting.
hints: The √3 factor (1.732) is essential for all three-phase calculations.
Star-delta starting reduces starting current to 1/3 of DOL (direct-on-line) delta, but also reduces starting torque to 1/3.
practice_problems.explanation: Motor current calculations are daily work for industrial electricians. Star-delta starting is the most common method for reducing starting current of large motors in SA industry, avoiding voltage dips that affect other equipment.
distractors: Star and delta give the same current
Starting current is always the same as running current

practice_problems.question: An Eskom bill shows a power factor penalty because the factory's power factor is 0.72. The total load is 200kVA. Calculate the required capacitor bank (in kVAr) to correct the power factor to 0.95.
practice_problems.answer: Current reactive power: Q1 = S x sin(arccos(0.72)) = 200 x sin(43.95°) = 200 x 0.694 = 138.8 kVAr. Active power: P = S x pf = 200 x 0.72 = 144 kW. Target reactive power at pf=0.95: Q2 = P x tan(arccos(0.95)) = 144 x tan(18.19°) = 144 x 0.329 = 47.4 kVAr. Required capacitor bank: Q_cap = Q1 - Q2 = 138.8 - 47.4 = 91.4 kVAr. Recommend a 100 kVAr automatic capacitor bank (with step controllers to avoid over-correction, which can cause leading power factor and voltage rise). Cost savings: eliminates the Eskom penalty (typically 1-2% of bill per 0.01 below 0.96) and reduces line current, freeing up transformer capacity.
hints: Power triangle: S² = P² + Q². Power factor = P/S = cos(φ).
Over-correction (leading power factor) is also penalised by Eskom -- use automatic step controllers.
practice_problems.explanation: Power factor correction is a lucrative service for electricians. Eskom penalises consumers with pf below 0.96. Capacitor banks pay for themselves within 6-12 months through reduced penalties and lower maximum demand charges.
distractors: Just install the biggest capacitor bank available
Power factor correction only benefits Eskom, not the customer
A single fixed capacitor is better than a stepped bank

practice_problems.question: You measure the following phase voltages at a three-phase DB: L1-N = 245V, L2-N = 228V, L3-N = 215V. Calculate the voltage imbalance percentage and explain the consequences.
practice_problems.answer: Average voltage = (245 + 228 + 215) / 3 = 229.3V. Maximum deviation from average = 245 - 229.3 = 15.7V. Voltage imbalance = (15.7 / 229.3) x 100 = 6.85%. This is EXCESSIVE. SANS 10142 and IEC standards recommend maximum 2-3% voltage imbalance. Consequences: (1) Three-phase motors draw unequal currents, causing overheating -- a 6.85% voltage imbalance can cause motor winding temperature to increase by 40-50%, dramatically reducing motor life. (2) The overloaded phase (L3 at 215V) draws higher current (I = P/V), potentially tripping breakers. (3) The high-voltage phase (L1 at 245V) may damage sensitive electronic equipment. Cause: likely unequal single-phase loading across phases. Solution: redistribute single-phase loads more evenly across the three phases.
hints: Voltage imbalance % = (max deviation from average / average) x 100.
Even small voltage imbalances cause disproportionate current imbalances in motors (roughly 6-10x the voltage imbalance percentage).
practice_problems.explanation: Phase balancing is a critical maintenance task in commercial and industrial installations. Unbalanced phases are one of the most common causes of premature motor failure and can also damage electronic equipment.
distractors: 6.85% imbalance is acceptable for most equipment
Voltage imbalance only matters for single-phase equipment
The solution is to install a bigger transformer

practice_problems.question: Explain the difference between a star (Y) and delta (Δ) transformer connection. A shopping centre needs 400V three-phase AND 230V single-phase from the same transformer. Which connection do they need on the secondary side?
practice_problems.answer: Star (Y) connection: Each winding connects from a common neutral point to a line terminal. Provides TWO voltages: line-to-line (400V) and line-to-neutral (230V). Has a neutral point available for single-phase loads. Delta (Δ) connection: Windings connect end-to-end forming a triangle. Provides only line-to-line voltage (400V). No neutral point available (unless a zig-zag earthing transformer is added). For the shopping centre: STAR connection on the secondary side is required. This provides 400V three-phase for large equipment (HVAC, lifts, kitchens) AND 230V single-phase (via any line to neutral) for lighting, plug sockets, and individual shops. The neutral allows single-phase loads to be distributed across the three phases for balance.
hints: The key difference is the NEUTRAL: star has one, delta does not.
In SA, 400V line-to-line and 230V line-to-neutral come from a star-connected secondary.
practice_problems.explanation: Understanding star vs delta is fundamental for specifying and connecting transformers. Most commercial buildings in SA use Dyn11 transformers (delta primary, star secondary with neutral) because they need both three-phase and single-phase supply.
distractors: Star and delta give the same voltages
You need two separate transformers for 400V and 230V

practice_problems.question: A three-phase motor suddenly starts running backwards after maintenance. What is the most likely cause, and how do you fix it? What damage could reverse rotation cause?
practice_problems.answer: Most likely cause: Two of the three phase connections were swapped during maintenance (e.g., L1 and L2 were reversed at the motor terminals or at the isolator). Three-phase motor rotation direction is determined by the phase sequence (R-W-B or L1-L2-L3). Swapping any two phases reverses the rotation. Fix: (1) Isolate the motor immediately. (2) Swap ANY two of the three phase connections back (at the motor terminal box or the isolator). (3) Bump-test the motor (brief power-on) to confirm correct rotation before reconnecting the driven equipment. Damage from reverse rotation: pumps can run dry or cavitate, fans blow air the wrong way (overheating equipment), conveyor belts run backwards (crushing/jamming hazards), compressors can be destroyed, and gearboxes can be damaged by reverse loading. Some equipment has anti-reverse rotation protection, but many do not.
hints: Only THREE-phase motors reverse from phase swapping. Single-phase motors do not.
Always mark the phase connections before disconnecting a motor for maintenance.
practice_problems.explanation: Phase sequence errors are one of the most common three-phase maintenance mistakes. A simple check with a phase rotation meter before reconnecting prevents potentially catastrophic damage to driven equipment.
distractors: The motor is faulty and needs rewinding
All three phases must be swapped to reverse rotation
Reverse rotation cannot damage equipment because motors have built-in protection

key_vocabulary.definition: Key concept in Three-Phase Power Systems
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Applied in Three-Phase Power Systems work

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for Three-Phase Power Systems
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
